title: Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club: What to Know Before You Play
bodyHtml:
Here's the short version: Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club is open to the public and playing as a 9-hole course in the redwoods at 16901 Big Basin Highway, with tee times you can book up to seven days out, an 18-hole disc golf course, and a restaurant and bar on site. The property lost its full 18-hole layout to the 2020 CZU fires and reopened after rebuilding the front nine, so if your last memory of the place is an 18-hole round, that part has changed. You can reach the Pro Shop at (831) 338-2111. Everything below was verified against the club's own site on July 18, 2026. Hours, rates, and course status change, so treat the links as the source of truth and call before a trip.

The course today: nine holes in the redwoods

The club has been here since 1961. It started as a nine-hole course, added a second nine in 1966 for a full 18, and was laid out by golf architect Jack Fleming. The 2020 CZU Lightning Complex fires burned through the property, and the course came back as a restored nine holes among the redwoods and small lakes. The club describes it as fresh air and quiet fairways, and that's a fair description of a short mountain round rather than a championship marathon.

The club posts its scorecard on its about page as an image. Because the current 9-hole par and yardage aren't listed in plain text on the site, this guide doesn't print a number that might be stale. Check the posted scorecard, or ask the Pro Shop when you book, so you know exactly what you're walking.

What it costs and how to book

Green fees are posted on the club's rates page. As posted, nine holes runs $24 on a weekday and $28 on weekends and holidays, with a same-day replay at $14 and $16. Twilight after 4 p.m. drops to $19 and $22, and super twilight after 5:30 p.m. drops again to $17 and $19. Juniors play for $12, and the Youth on Course rate is $5 with ID. A power cart for nine holes is $16 per person, and a pull cart is $5. The rates page carries no dated stamp, so confirm the current price when you reserve.

Book online through the club's EZLinks tee sheet, where tee times open up to seven days in advance, or call the Pro Shop at (831) 338-2111. The Pro Shop is open 7 a.m. to 6 p.m. daily.

Disc golf on the back of the property

If you throw discs instead of swinging clubs, the club runs an 18-hole disc golf course called Redwood Run, with an option to play 27, open to the public at $12 per player from 7 a.m. to 6 p.m. daily. Check in at the clubhouse before you play. A few house rules to know before you go: shirts and shoes are required, tank tops aren't allowed, and dogs aren't permitted on the course. Private homes border several holes, so if a disc sails onto someone's property, get the homeowner's okay before you go looking for it.

The restaurant and bar

There's a full bar and restaurant on site, and the hours shift by day, so it's worth reading before you count on a meal. As the club posts them: the bar is open Monday, Wednesday, and Thursday from 1 to 9 p.m. with no kitchen those days; Tuesday is Taco Tuesday with the kitchen serving 11 a.m. to 8 p.m. and the bar open 11 to 9; Friday the bar runs 11 to 9 with the menu 11 to 7; Saturday the bar opens at 8 a.m. and the menu runs 11 to 7; and Sunday brings breakfast from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. plus the regular menu 11 to 3, with the bar open from 8 a.m. The club notes the bar reserves the right to close earlier or later, so a quick call to (831) 338-2111 saves a wasted drive on a slow day.

Events, tournaments, and private groups

The club has event facilities and books golf outings. Its events page pitches everything from a large corporate retreat to a small group on a golf getaway, and it runs tournaments. It doesn't spell out wedding or banquet details on the site, so if you're thinking about a private event, call the club directly at (831) 338-2111 or email hello@bouldercreekgolf.com to ask what they can host and what a date would involve.

Where to stay if you're making a weekend of it

There's no lodging at the club itself. The club's own lodging page points visitors to the Brookdale Lodge, a historic redwood inn a few minutes down Highway 9 in Brookdale. If you'd rather stay closer to downtown Boulder Creek, our guide to where to stay covers the local options.

Getting there

The club sits at 16901 Big Basin Highway, on the Big Basin Way side of town heading toward the state park. It's a short drive from downtown Boulder Creek, and the last stretch is classic mountain road, so give yourself a few extra minutes and watch for cyclists and one-lane pinch points. If you're new to driving these roads, our mountain roads guide is worth a read.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club open to the public?

Yes. It's a public course, and anyone can book a tee time online through the club's EZLinks page up to seven days ahead or by calling the Pro Shop at (831) 338-2111. The disc golf course is public too.

How many holes is the golf course now?

Nine. The property was an 18-hole course before the 2020 CZU fires, and it reopened after rebuilding the front nine. The separate disc golf course is 18 holes with an option to play 27.

How much does it cost to play nine holes?

As posted on the club's rates page, $24 on a weekday and $28 on weekends and holidays, with cheaper twilight, junior, and replay rates. The page isn't dated, so confirm the current fee when you book.

Does the golf club have a restaurant?

Yes, there's a full bar and restaurant on site, but the kitchen isn't open every day. Dining runs Tuesday and Friday through Sunday, with the bar open more days than the kitchen. Call (831) 338-2111 to confirm before you rely on a meal.

Can you hold a wedding or private event there?

The club has event facilities and books tournaments, corporate retreats, and golf outings. It doesn't publish wedding or banquet details on its site, so call the club to ask what private events it can host.
seoTitle: Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club: Play, Dine, Visit
metaDescription: Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club is open as a 9-hole redwood course with disc golf, a restaurant, and a bar. Rates, hours, booking, and what to know before you go.
excerpt: Boulder Creek Golf & Country Club is open as a 9-hole redwood course with disc golf, a restaurant, and a bar. Rates, hours, booking, and what to know before you go.

title: Is Boulder Creek a Good Place to Live? The Real Trade-offs
bodyHtml:
Here's a straight answer instead of a sales pitch: Boulder Creek is a wonderful place to live if you want redwoods, quiet, and a real community, and you go in clear-eyed about the trade-offs. The big ones are the cost and difficulty of fire insurance, one main road in and out, a real commute to most jobs, and a long drive for a big grocery run or a late-night pharmacy. None of that is a dealbreaker for the people who love it here. All of it matters more than a listing photo will tell you. This piece walks through the full ledger, both sides, with the numbers checked against public sources as of July 2026. If you're already leaning yes, our new resident quick-start guide covers the week-one logistics.

What homes actually cost

Boulder Creek is expensive, like most of the Santa Cruz Mountains, but not Silicon Valley expensive. The exact number depends on who's counting and what kind of home. As of mid-2026, Redfin put the median sale price around $678,000 as of May 2026, up a few percent year over year, while Zillow's typical home value sat higher, near $785,000. Single-family-only data from the local MLS ran lower, closer to $571,000 in the same window. Call it roughly $600,000 to $800,000 depending on the source, the month, and whether you're looking at a cabin or a full house on usable land. Prices move, so treat those as a snapshot, not a quote, and check the live figures before you make any decision.

What the median hides is the range. A small off-grid-ish cabin on a steep lot is a very different purchase from a rebuilt home on a sunny flat parcel, and the gap between them is wide up here. Condition, sun exposure, water source, and road access swing prices more than square footage does.

The insurance problem is real, and you have to plan for it

This is the part realtor blogs skip. Boulder Creek sits in a high fire-risk area, and standard home insurers have pulled back hard across the Santa Cruz Mountains. Many homeowners here have ended up on the California FAIR Plan, which the plan itself describes as "an insurer of last resort, established by statute to provide basic property insurance to Californians statewide when no other option is reasonably available." It's a backstop, not a bargain, and it covers less than a standard policy.

It's also getting pricier. As Lookout Santa Cruz reported in October 2025, the FAIR Plan proposed rate increases that would hit Boulder Creek's 95006 ZIP code with a roughly 47.7% jump for policy renewals after April 1, 2026, among the steepest in a county where nearly 11,000 residents rely on the plan. Budget for insurance as a serious line item, not a rounding error, and get real quotes for a specific address before you commit, because two homes a mile apart can price very differently.

One protection worth knowing: under state law, after the Governor declares a wildfire emergency, insurers can't cancel or non-renew residential policies for wildfire risk in the affected ZIP codes for one year from the declaration. That buys time, but it isn't a permanent fix, and the underlying market is the thing to plan around.

The commute is a real commute

If your job is over the hill, know what you're signing up for. Typical drive times from Boulder Creek run about 30 minutes to Santa Cruz, 40 to 50 minutes to San Jose by way of Highway 9 and Highway 17, and 50 to 60 minutes up to the Redwood City area. Those are good-day estimates. Highway 17 backs up, and winter weather or a downed tree on Highway 9 can turn any of them into something longer. People do these commutes daily and make peace with them, but it's worth driving your actual route at your actual hour before you decide, not on a quiet Sunday.

Remote and hybrid work makes Boulder Creek far more livable than it was a decade ago, which is a big reason people can choose the redwoods and still keep a valley job.

One way in, one way out

Boulder Creek is a mountain town with limited road access, and that shapes daily life and emergencies alike. During the 2020 CZU Lightning Complex fire, the whole town was evacuated along with Ben Lomond, Felton, and Lompico, and Highway 9 was a critical line. When thousands of people need to leave at once on a narrow mountain highway, it's slow, and that's the reality you accept living here.

The practical answer isn't fear, it's preparation. Sign up for CruzAware emergency alerts the day you move in, keep a go-bag ready in fire season, and know your routes. Our guides to a power outage plan and a winter storm checklist cover the rest. Living somewhere with one main road means the habits that feel like overkill in the suburbs are just normal here.

The "30 minutes for a carton of eggs" question

You'll hear this one a lot, and it's half true. There's no big-box store, no 24-hour anything, and a Costco run or a specific hardware part can mean a drive down to Scotts Valley or Santa Cruz. If you're used to a supermarket on every corner, that adjustment is real.

The half that isn't true: you can cover most everyday needs without leaving town. Johnnie's Super Market and Wild Roots Market handle real grocery shopping, there's a local pharmacy and a hardware store, and downtown has coffee, restaurants, and the basics. Our guide to essential shops in Boulder Creek maps out what's actually here. You learn to keep a fuller pantry and to batch your valley errands, and after a while the extra planning just becomes how you live.

The upside nobody has to sell you

Here's why people stay. You live in the redwoods, with cool green air and quiet that city transplants describe as almost startling at first. The pace is slower, the night sky is dark, and the river and Big Basin are minutes away. The community is genuinely tight, the kind of place where neighbors check on each other during outages and the whole town turns out for the Fourth of July parade. It's small, it's a little rough around the edges, and the people who love Boulder Creek love exactly that.

So is it a good place to live? If you want redwoods and community more than convenience, and you plan realistically for insurance, the commute, and the road, yes. If you need a short commute, cheap coverage, and a store around every corner, it'll wear on you. Only you can weigh that, and the point of this guide is to let you weigh it with real numbers instead of a brochure.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does a house cost in Boulder Creek?

As of mid-2026, roughly $600,000 to $800,000 depending on the source and the type of home, with single-family figures on the lower end and Zillow's typical-value estimate on the higher end. Cabins and steep lots run cheaper, and full homes on good land run more. Prices move, so check current figures before you rely on a number.

Is it hard to get home insurance in Boulder Creek?

Yes. It's a high fire-risk area, and many homeowners rely on the California FAIR Plan, the state's insurer of last resort, which costs more and covers less than a standard policy. FAIR Plan rates for the 95006 ZIP were proposed to rise sharply for renewals after April 2026. Get a real quote for the specific address before you buy.

How long is the commute from Boulder Creek?

Typically about 30 minutes to Santa Cruz, 40 to 50 minutes to San Jose, and 50 to 60 minutes to the Redwood City area on a good day. Highway 17 traffic and winter weather can make it longer, so test your real route at your real commute time.

Is Boulder Creek safe from wildfires?

It's a fire-prone area, and the 2020 CZU fire evacuated the whole town, so the reality is that you live with real risk and prepare for it. Sign up for CruzAware alerts, keep a go-bag in fire season, and know your evacuation routes. Preparation, not luck, is what makes it manageable.

Do you have to drive far for groceries?

Not for everyday shopping. Johnnie's Super Market and Wild Roots Market cover real groceries in town, and there's a local pharmacy and hardware store. You'll drive down to Scotts Valley or Santa Cruz for a big-box store or a Costco run, so most locals keep a fuller pantry and batch their valley errands.
seoTitle: Is Boulder Creek, CA a Good Place to Live? A Local Guide
metaDescription: A straight local answer on living in Boulder Creek, CA: real home prices, the fire-insurance problem, commute times, the one-road evacuation reality, and the redwood upside.
excerpt: A straight local answer on living in Boulder Creek, CA: real home prices, the fire-insurance problem, commute times, the one-road evacuation reality, and the redwood upside.

title: Healthcare Near Boulder Creek: Pharmacy, Urgent Care, and the Nearest ER
bodyHtml:
The plain reality first: there is no hospital, emergency room, or urgent care inside the San Lorenzo Valley. Boulder Creek has a pharmacy in town and a community primary-care clinic one town south in Ben Lomond, but for a walk-in urgent care you're driving to Scotts Valley, and for a true emergency room you're driving to Santa Cruz. If someone's having a real emergency, call 911 and let them come to you. Don't drive yourself over Highway 9 and Highway 17. Everything below was verified against official sources on July 18, 2026. This guide doesn't diagnose anything or promise that any provider can see you; it just tells you plainly where care is and how far. Hours and details change, so treat each link as the source of truth.

Call 911 first for a real emergency

Some things don't wait for a drive to Santa Cruz. Call 911 right away for chest pain or pressure, signs of a stroke (face drooping, arm weakness, or slurred and difficult speech), severe or uncontrolled bleeding, or trouble breathing. Up here, where the nearest ER is a good half hour away on a clear day, that call matters more than it did wherever you lived before. Paramedics can start care at your door and get you to the right hospital faster than you can, and they can radio ahead. Don't talk yourself out of it, and don't get behind the wheel with a sick passenger if it's serious.

For a possible poisoning, an accidental overdose, or a "what did the toddler just swallow" moment, the California Poison Control System is free, staffed by pharmacists and physicians, and open 24 hours a day at 1-800-222-1222. It's worth putting in your phone right now.

The pharmacy in town

You don't have to leave Boulder Creek to fill a prescription. Boulder Creek Pharmacy is an independent pharmacy at 13081 Central Avenue, open weekdays 9 a.m. to 6 p.m. and Saturday 10 a.m. to 4 p.m., closed Sunday. The number is 831-338-2144.

The part that actually matters when you live up a long driveway: they deliver free throughout the San Lorenzo Valley, and they've extended complimentary delivery to Felton and Scotts Valley for former Rite Aid customers. For anyone who'd rather not drive down the hill sick, or who's caring for someone who can't, a local pharmacy that brings the medicine to you is a genuine asset.

Primary care in the valley

For the ordinary stuff, checkups, a nagging cough, managing an ongoing condition, there's a community clinic without leaving the valley. Santa Cruz Mountain Health Center, run by the nonprofit Santa Cruz Community Health, sits at 231 Main Street, Suite A, in Ben Lomond, a few minutes south on Highway 9. It's open weekdays 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. (closed 11 to noon for lunch), with Saturday morning hours offered at the group's Live Oak location. The number is 831-427-3500.

They handle primary care for adults and kids, prenatal care, and behavioral health, and they accept Medi-Cal, Medicare, and most commercial plans, with a sliding scale for people without insurance. It runs by appointment, so call to book rather than dropping in. One important thing to be clear about: this is a primary-care clinic, not an urgent care and not an emergency room. It's the right call for a scheduled visit or an ongoing health need, not for a middle-of-the-night crisis.

The nearest urgent care

When it's more than a phone call to the doctor but less than a 911 emergency, a sprain, a deep cut that might need stitches, a fever that won't quit, you're driving down the hill for a walk-in urgent care. There isn't one in the valley, and Sutter Health closed its Scotts Valley walk-in urgent care in 2021, so plan on a longer drive than you might expect.

For anyone, the nearest walk-in urgent care open to the general public is Doctors on Duty at 615 Ocean Street in Santa Cruz, open weekdays 8 a.m. to 6 p.m. and weekends 8 a.m. to 2 p.m., at 831-425-7991. That's roughly a thirty-minute drive down Highway 9 and Highway 17. Call before you go, especially late in the day, since hours shift.

If you're a Kaiser Permanente member, Kaiser runs urgent care closer, at its Scotts Valley Medical Offices, 5615 Scotts Valley Drive, about twenty minutes down Highway 9, at 831-430-2700, with a 24-hour advice line for members at 831-430-2740. That urgent care is for Kaiser members only, and its hours vary, so check the current schedule through Kaiser before heading over.

The nearest emergency room

For anything that needs a real emergency room, broken bones, deep wounds, severe pain, a bad reaction, chest or breathing trouble that a paramedic tells you to bring in, the closest ER is in Santa Cruz. Dignity Health's Dominican Hospital has an emergency room at 1555 Soquel Drive that's open 24 hours a day, every day, with specially trained teams for stroke and heart care. The hospital's number is 831-462-7700. From Boulder Creek, plan on thirty to forty minutes down Highway 9 and Highway 17, longer in traffic or bad weather.

There's a second 24-hour ER farther south at Watsonville Community Hospital, 75 Nielson Street, at 831-724-4741. It's a longer drive, closer to an hour, so for most Boulder Creek residents Dominican is the practical choice. Either way, if it's a genuine emergency, the fastest route to an ER is often the ambulance, not your own car. Call 911.

Telehealth and the nurse-line question

There's no telehealth service that belongs to the valley, but you may already have one and not know it. Most health plans include a nurse advice line and video visits, which are the fastest way to get a real answer at 10 p.m. without a drive. Kaiser members have that 24-hour advice line at 831-430-2740. Sutter and PAMF patients can do video visits through My Health Online. Check your insurance card and your plan's app; the option is usually there, and it can save you a trip down the hill or tell you plainly when it's time to make one.

The bottom line on distance

None of this is meant to scare anyone off mountain living. It's just the trade you make for the redwoods: care is real, but it's down the hill. Keep the pharmacy's number and the poison line in your phone, know that walk-in urgent care and the ER are both down in Santa Cruz (Kaiser members have urgent care closer, in Scotts Valley), and keep a small first-aid kit and any critical medications stocked at home so a minor thing doesn't become a midnight drive. For the serious stuff, 911 is always the right first move.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there an urgent care in Boulder Creek?

No. There's no urgent care, emergency room, or hospital anywhere in the San Lorenzo Valley. The nearest walk-in urgent care open to everyone is Doctors on Duty in Santa Cruz, about thirty minutes down the hill; Kaiser members have one closer, in Scotts Valley. The nearest 24-hour ER is Dominican Hospital in Santa Cruz.

Where's the closest emergency room to Boulder Creek?

Dignity Health's Dominican Hospital at 1555 Soquel Drive in Santa Cruz has a 24-hour ER, roughly thirty to forty minutes away. Watsonville Community Hospital also has a 24-hour ER, but it's a longer drive. For a true emergency, call 911 rather than driving yourself.

Does the Boulder Creek pharmacy deliver?

Yes. Boulder Creek Pharmacy, at 13081 Central Avenue, delivers free throughout the San Lorenzo Valley, and it has extended free delivery to Felton and Scotts Valley for former Rite Aid customers. Call 831-338-2144.

Is there a doctor's office in the valley?

Yes. Santa Cruz Mountain Health Center in Ben Lomond offers primary care for adults and children, takes Medi-Cal and most insurance, and has a sliding scale for the uninsured. It's appointment-based at 831-427-3500. It's a primary-care clinic, not an urgent care or ER.

What should I do for a poisoning or if my kid swallows something?

Call the California Poison Control System at 1-800-222-1222. It's free, open 24 hours, and staffed by pharmacists and physicians. If the person is unconscious, not breathing, or having a seizure, call 911 first.
seoTitle: Healthcare Near Boulder Creek, CA: Urgent Care and ER
metaDescription: There's no ER or urgent care in the San Lorenzo Valley. Here's the in-town pharmacy, the nearest walk-in urgent care in Santa Cruz, and the closest 24/7 ER.
excerpt: There's no ER or urgent care in the San Lorenzo Valley. Here's the in-town pharmacy, the nearest walk-in urgent care in Santa Cruz, and the closest 24/7 ER.

title: Vets and Pet Services In and Around Boulder Creek
bodyHtml:
Good news for a mountain town: you don't have to leave Boulder Creek for a vet. There's a full-service veterinary clinic right on Highway 9 in town, and another a few minutes south in Felton, so routine care, shots, dental work, and daytime sick visits are all handled close to home. The one gap is overnight emergencies. Neither valley clinic is a 24-hour hospital, so for a middle-of-the-night crisis you're driving to Santa Cruz. Below is who does what, grouped by need, with the details we could confirm. Vet clinics and the emergency hospital were verified against their own websites on July 18, 2026; grooming and boarding come from our directory, verified in mid-July. Hours change, so call ahead.

The vet in town

Boulder Creek Veterinary Clinic is the full-service clinic right in town at 12870 Highway 9, reachable at 831-338-7205. It's open Monday and Wednesday 8 a.m. to 8 p.m., Tuesday, Thursday, and Friday 8 a.m. to 6 p.m., and Saturday 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., closed Sunday. Those two late nights a week are genuinely handy in a valley where most things close early.

They cover the whole range of companion-animal care: puppy and kitten wellness, spay and neuter, dental cleanings with digital x-rays, laser therapy, in-house lab work, ultrasound, and even exotic pets, plus therapeutic bathing and boarding. Their site says they're staffed and equipped to handle emergencies during open hours, so if something happens on a weekday, call them first. Just know that "emergencies during open hours" isn't the same as an overnight ER, which is covered further down.

The vet in Felton

A short drive south, Felton Veterinary Hospital at 5996 Highway 9 is the valley's other clinic, at 831-335-3466. It's open weekdays 8 a.m. to 6 p.m. and Saturday 8 a.m. to 2 p.m.

Along with preventive care, diagnostics, dentistry, surgery, and exotics, they also offer boarding and grooming, which makes them a convenient one-stop if your dog needs a checkup and a bath in the same trip. It's about fifteen minutes down Highway 9 from Boulder Creek, so it's a reasonable backup if the in-town clinic is booked or closed on the day you need them.

After-hours and overnight emergencies

Here's the honest limit of valley care: there's no 24-hour animal hospital in the San Lorenzo Valley. If your pet has an emergency at night, on a Sunday, or after the local clinics close, the nearest round-the-clock animal ER is Pacific and Santa Cruz Veterinary Specialists at 2585 Soquel Drive in Santa Cruz. They're open 24 hours a day, seven days a week, all year, at 831-475-5400.

They take walk-ins, and while they recommend calling ahead so their team can prepare, they know emergencies don't wait. From Boulder Creek, plan on thirty to forty minutes down Highway 9 and Highway 17. It's worth putting that number and address in your phone now, before you need it at 2 a.m. with a scared animal in the back seat.

Grooming

For grooming, you've got a few options within a short drive. Dapper Dogs and Divas is an appointment-based dog grooming shop at 6144 Highway 9, Unit B, in Felton, run by groomer Jackson Hamilton, open Tuesday through Saturday 8 a.m. to 4 p.m., at 831-216-6462.

In Ben Lomond, Mountain Paw Spa is a home-based grooming business run by Kristin Miller, offering full grooms, bath and brush, nail trims, and ear cleaning by appointment at 831-266-7998. Since it works out of a home, there's no walk-in storefront; book ahead. And as noted above, Felton Veterinary Hospital offers grooming alongside its boarding, which is handy if you'd rather keep everything under one roof.

Boarding, daycare, and pet sitting

If you're heading out of town, Camp Happy Tails covers the San Lorenzo Valley with doggy daycare, boarding, grooming, and in-home pet sitting. It's a licensed, bonded, and insured operation reachable at 831-247-8245. Because it serves the valley rather than running a public storefront, call to arrange details.

Both valley vet clinics board too. Boulder Creek Veterinary Clinic offers boarding in town, and Felton Veterinary Hospital offers supervised dog and cat boarding, which can be reassuring if your pet has a medical condition and you'd rather it stay somewhere with a vet on site.

Wildlife: injured, sick, or orphaned wild animals

This is redwood country, so sooner or later you'll find a wild animal that seems to be in trouble, a fledgling bird, a fawn, an injured raccoon. Wildlife doesn't go to the vet or the pound. It goes to Native Animal Rescue, the licensed nonprofit that cares for Santa Cruz County's sick, injured, and orphaned wildlife, at their hotline, 831-462-0726.

Their guidance is worth following: not every wild animal actually needs rescue, and picking one up can do more harm than good. Take a photo, call the hotline first, and let their wildlife dispatcher tell you what to do before you touch anything. Their intake is at 1855 17th Avenue in Santa Cruz, but the call comes first.

Strays, lost pets, and animal control

For a stray dog, a lost cat, or a domestic animal in trouble, that's the Santa Cruz County Animal Shelter at 831-454-7224. The main shelter is at 1001 Rodriguez Street in Santa Cruz, and because Boulder Creek is unincorporated county, the shelter and its animal control officers serve our area. They're who you call to report a stray, look for a lost pet, or ask about a dangerous or aggressive animal.

The shelter also points wildlife calls to Native Animal Rescue and to Wildlife Emergency Services at 831-498-9453. As a rule of thumb: if it's someone's pet, or should be, call the county shelter; if it's wild, call Native Animal Rescue first.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is there a vet in Boulder Creek?

Yes. Boulder Creek Veterinary Clinic is a full-service companion-animal clinic in town at 12870 Highway 9, open six days a week with two late nights, at 831-338-7205. Felton Veterinary Hospital is a second option about fifteen minutes south on Highway 9.

Where do I take my pet for an emergency at night?

Neither valley clinic is open 24 hours. The nearest round-the-clock animal ER is Pacific and Santa Cruz Veterinary Specialists at 2585 Soquel Drive in Santa Cruz, open 24/7 at 831-475-5400, about thirty to forty minutes away. They take walk-ins but appreciate a call ahead.

Who do I call about an injured wild animal?

Native Animal Rescue, at 831-462-0726. They handle the county's sick, injured, and orphaned wildlife. Take a photo and call before you try to help, since not every animal needs rescue and handling can hurt. Wildlife doesn't go to a vet clinic or the animal shelter.

What about a stray or lost dog?

Call the Santa Cruz County Animal Shelter at 831-454-7224. Boulder Creek is unincorporated county, so the shelter and its animal control officers cover our area for strays, lost and found pets, and dangerous-animal reports.

Where can I get my dog groomed near Boulder Creek?

Dapper Dogs and Divas in Felton and Mountain Paw Spa in Ben Lomond both groom by appointment, and Felton Veterinary Hospital offers grooming with its boarding. Camp Happy Tails also grooms as part of its valley pet-care services. Call ahead at any of them, since these are small, appointment-driven shops.
seoTitle: Vets and Pet Services Near Boulder Creek, CA
metaDescription: There's a full-service vet in Boulder Creek and another in Felton. Plus the nearest 24-hour animal ER, grooming, boarding, and who to call for wildlife.
excerpt: There's a full-service vet in Boulder Creek and another in Felton. Plus the nearest 24-hour animal ER, grooming, boarding, and who to call for wildlife.

title: How Boulder Creek Prepares for a Wildfire Evacuation
bodyHtml:
If a wildfire threatens Boulder Creek, the decision to evacuate is not one you'll read here. Official evacuation warnings and orders come from the Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office and CAL FIRE, and they reach you through CruzAware, the county's alert system. This site can't tell you a road is clear, a zone is safe, or that it's fine to stay. What it can do is help you get ready now, so that when an alert comes, you already know your zone, you have a bag packed, and you're not making big decisions for the first time in the dark. For anything happening right now, a fire you can see or smell, call 911. Everything below is for the calm days before, which are the only days to do this.

Know Your Zone Before There's Ever a Fire

Santa Cruz County divides the whole county into small evacuation zones, and every address sits in one. During an emergency, the Sheriff and fire agencies issue warnings and orders zone by zone, so the single most useful thing you can do ahead of time is learn yours.

Look it up at community.zonehaven.com, the county's evacuation map, or through the county's "Know Your Zone" page. Type in your address, and the map shows your Zone ID along with the fire and law enforcement agencies that cover you. Boulder Creek zones start with the letters BOU, followed by a number, something like BOU-E028. Write yours down. Learn the zones your kids' school, your work, and your parents' place fall in too, because an order for one zone tells you nothing about the next one over.

Knowing your zone turns a chaotic alert into a clear one. When an order says "BOU-E0-whatever is under an evacuation order," you'll know in a second whether that's you.

The Difference Between a Warning and an Order

Two words do a lot of work in an evacuation, and they don't mean the same thing.

An evacuation warning means there's a potential threat to your area. It's the county telling you to get ready to go and to leave now if you need extra time. The county's own guidance is direct on this point: people with disabilities, access or functional needs, anyone who needs longer to get out, and anyone with pets or livestock should leave when the warning comes, not wait for the order. A warning is your cue to load the car, not to keep an eye on it.

An evacuation order means the threat is immediate and it's time to leave now. When an order is issued for your zone, go. Don't stop to pack more, don't wait to see how it develops, and don't drive around road barricades. In a fast-moving mountain fire there may be no time for anyone to knock on your door, so the order is the knock.

You'll also sometimes hear "shelter in place," which means stay where you are because leaving is riskier than staying put. That instruction, like the others, comes from officials, not from this site. The safe habit is simple: when the county tells you to go, go early and go without arguing with it.

Where the Real Orders and Information Come From

In an emergency, rumors move faster than fire. A neighbor's group text, a screenshot of a screenshot, a well-meaning post with no source behind it: none of that is an evacuation order. Get your information from the places that issue it.

  • CruzAware is the county's alert system and the main way evacuation warnings and orders reach your phone by text, call, and email. If you do nothing else after reading this, sign up at cruzaware.org. Register more than one contact method and more than one address. We have a full walkthrough in our CruzAware setup guide.
  • The community.zonehaven.com map shows the current status of every zone during an incident, color-coded, so you can see whether your zone is under a warning or an order.
  • The Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office and CAL FIRE are the agencies that make and announce evacuation decisions. CAL FIRE's Active Incidents page tracks fires across the state.
  • 911 is for reporting a fire or an emergency and for a life-threatening situation right now. It is not a general information line, so keep it clear for people who need it.

No alert system is perfect. Phones lose signal, texts fail, and power goes out, which is common in a mountain fire. That's exactly why you sign up for more than one channel and why knowing your zone and your plan ahead of time matters so much. Treat every alert as one layer, not the whole safety net.

The Highway 9 Reality: Know More Than One Way Out

Boulder Creek is a canyon town with limited roads, and most traffic funnels onto Highway 9. In a large evacuation, that road carries a lot of cars at once, and it can also be the road firefighters need. This is not a reason to panic. It's a reason to leave early and to know your options before you need them.

Learn more than one route away from your home, and drive them on an ordinary day so they're familiar. Keep at least half a tank of gas in the car through fire season, because a full evacuation is no time to be hunting for an open station. Back your car into the driveway or park it facing out, so you're ready to pull away without a three-point turn.

One thing this site will never do is map a "safe route" out of town, because no one can promise a given road is passable or clear when a fire is moving. Conditions change by the minute, and only the officials running the incident have the full picture. When an order comes, follow the routes and directions they give you, obey road closures and barricades, and if you're told a road is closed, believe it. The whole point of leaving early is to be gone before the roads are the problem.

Leave Early. Don't Wait to Be Sure

The hardest part of evacuating is deciding to do it. It always feels too soon, right up until it's too late. Every fire agency says the same thing: if you feel at risk, don't wait for a mandatory order, and if an order comes, don't wait at all.

Leaving early is safer for you and for everyone else. It keeps the roads clearer for the people still coming out behind you and for the trucks coming in. It gives you time to move calmly instead of racing. And it means a stressful evening at a friend's house instead of being caught on the road. If you ever find yourself weighing whether it's "bad enough" to go, that hesitation is your answer. Go.

If you have animals, medical needs, young kids, or anyone in the house who moves slowly, build your plan around leaving at the warning stage, not the order. The extra hour is the whole game.

Get Your Home and Your Go-Bag Ready Now

Two kinds of preparation make the biggest difference, and both are done long before any smoke: hardening your home and space around it, and having a bag ready to grab.

Defensible space and home hardening. CAL FIRE's Ready for Wildfire program lays out the buffer zones around a house, from the ember-resistant five feet closest to the walls out to 100 feet, plus the home-hardening details like vents, gutters, roofs, and decks that decide whether flying embers find a way in. We've pulled all of it into a printable home-prep and defensible-space checklist so you can walk your property with it in hand. Some of this work, especially limbing up and removing trees near the house, is a job for a professional. Our guide to hiring local tree services can help you find someone reputable up here.

Your go-bag. A go-bag is the kit you can grab and be out the door with in two minutes. It holds a few days of essentials, your medications, copies of key documents, phone chargers, cash, and supplies for your pets. Our printable go-bag checklist, built from CAL FIRE and American Red Cross guidance, lays it out item by item. Pack it once, keep it by the door, and check it a couple of times a year.

Do these two things and you've handled most of what's actually in your control. Keep sturdy shoes and a flashlight by your bed, too, in case an alert comes at 2 a.m.

Pets and Livestock

Animals are part of the plan, and the plan has to account for them before an emergency, not during one.

For pets, keep a carrier for each one, and store their food, water, medications, and vaccination records with your go-bag. Make sure collars and ID tags are current. When a warning comes, bring pets inside and keep them close so you're not chasing a scared cat under the porch when it's time to leave.

Livestock and horses take more lead time, which is the main reason to leave at the warning, not the order. Arrange transport ahead of season if you don't have your own trailer, and know where you'd take animals, whether that's a fairground, a friend's property, or a boarding facility out of the area. CAL FIRE's Go Evacuation Guide has detailed livestock and pet checklists worth reading before you need them. Firefighters focus on the fire and may not be able to move your animals, so their safety comes down to your own advance planning.

Why This Matters So Much Here

Boulder Creek doesn't need to be told that wildfire is real. In August 2020, the CZU Lightning Complex burned through the Santa Cruz Mountains, and our community was among the hardest hit. According to CAL FIRE, the fire covered about 86,000 acres before it was contained that September, and it destroyed 1,490 structures, most of them homes, and took one life. Many neighbors are still rebuilding, and many more carry the memory of that evacuation.

We raise this plainly and without drama because the lesson from CZU is a practical one, not a fearful one. The people who had already looked up their zone, packed a bag, and decided in advance that they'd leave early were the ones who moved fastest and with the least panic when it counted. Preparation didn't stop the fire, but it gave people room to make good decisions. That's the whole aim of this page: not to frighten anyone, and never to tell you it's safe, but to help you be ready and to know exactly where to look when the county speaks.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find my evacuation zone in Boulder Creek?

Go to community.zonehaven.com and type in your address. The map shows your Zone ID, which for Boulder Creek starts with BOU, along with the agencies that cover you. Look up your home, work, and family members' addresses now, while it's calm, and write the zones down.

Who actually issues evacuation orders here?

The Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office and CAL FIRE make and announce evacuation decisions. They reach you mainly through CruzAware alerts and the community.zonehaven.com map. This site does not issue orders and can't tell you it's safe to stay or go. For a fire or an emergency right now, call 911.

What's the difference between an evacuation warning and an order?

A warning means a potential threat and it's time to get ready and leave now if you need extra time, have animals, or move slowly. An order means an immediate threat and it's time to leave now, without stopping to pack more. When your zone is under an order, go.

Should I wait for an official order before I leave?

No. Every fire agency advises leaving before a mandatory order if you feel at risk, and leaving immediately once an order is issued. Leaving early keeps you safer and keeps the roads clear for others and for firefighters. If you're unsure whether it's time, that uncertainty is your cue to go.

What should I do about the one road out of town?

Know more than one way out and drive your routes on ordinary days so they're familiar. Keep at least half a tank of gas through fire season and park facing out. In an actual evacuation, follow the routes officials give you and obey every road closure. This site won't map a "safe route," because no one can promise a road is clear when a fire is moving.

How do I get ready before fire season?

Sign up for CruzAware, look up your zone, pack a go-bag, and work through defensible space and home hardening around your house. Our printable go-bag checklist and home-prep checklist walk through both, and our CruzAware setup guide covers the alert signup step by step.
seoTitle: Boulder Creek Wildfire Evacuation: How to Prepare
metaDescription: In a wildfire, official evacuation orders come from the Sheriff and CAL FIRE through CruzAware, not this site. Here's how Boulder Creek residents get ready and know where to look.
excerpt: In a wildfire, official evacuation orders come from the Sheriff and CAL FIRE through CruzAware, not this site. Here's how Boulder Creek residents get ready and know where to look.

title: Earthquakes in the Santa Cruz Mountains: A Boulder Creek Preparedness Guide
bodyHtml:
If you searched "Boulder Creek earthquake" or "San Andreas Fault Santa Cruz Mountains," here's the lay of the land and what to do about it. Yes, one of the most famous faults on Earth runs through these mountains, and yes, the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake started just up the ridge from here. No one, including the U.S. Geological Survey, can tell you when the next big one will hit, so the useful move isn't worrying about the timing. It's doing the handful of things that make your household safer whenever it comes: secure the heavy stuff, store water and supplies, learn Drop, Cover, and Hold On, and sign up for alerts. Most of it takes a weekend, and none of it requires you to become a seismologist.

We live in earthquake country. That's not a reason to be scared. It's a reason to be ready, the same way we get ready for winter storms and fire season.

The San Andreas Fault Runs Through These Mountains

The San Andreas is not a distant California abstraction up here. The USGS describes the San Andreas Fault Zone as "the main part of the boundary between the Pacific tectonic plate on the west side and the North American plate on the east side." Those two plates grind past each other at a rate the USGS puts at "50 mm/yr (about 2 inches/yr)." That steady motion is what loads the fault with the strain that earthquakes eventually release.

The stretch nearest us has a name. The USGS calls it the Santa Cruz Mountains section, and says it "stretches 62 km (39 mi) from Los Gatos (near San Jose) to San Juan Bautista, CA and was last ruptured in the famous 1906 San Francisco earthquake." Boulder Creek sits inside that band of country. Other active faults, including the Hayward and Calaveras, run through the wider Bay Area too, which is part of why this region is studied as closely as it is.

One point is worth holding onto: proximity to a fault tells you an earthquake is possible, not that one is imminent. Nobody can predict earthquakes. If you want to understand the specific hazard for your parcel, the authority is the USGS, not a blog and not a neighbor's hunch. Their earthquake hazards program publishes the maps and forecasts that professionals rely on, and it's the right place to send any question that starts with "how likely is."

What the 1989 Loma Prieta Earthquake Tells Us

The Loma Prieta earthquake is local history, not ancient history, and it's the clearest picture we have of what a major quake does to this terrain. According to the USGS, "On October 17, 1989, at 5:04 PM (PDT), a magnitude 6.9 earthquake" struck the region. The USGS places the epicenter "near Loma Prieta peak in the Santa Cruz Mountains, approximately 9 miles northeast of Santa Cruz and 60 miles southeast of San Francisco." That peak is in our mountains, southeast of Boulder Creek.

The toll was serious. The USGS records "63 deaths, 3757 injuries, and an estimated $6 billion in property damage," and calls it "the strongest earthquake to hit the San Francisco Bay region since the M 7.9 Great San Francisco earthquake of 1906." Longtime residents remember the collapsed section of the Bay Bridge and the Cypress Street Viaduct in Oakland, but the shaking was felt hardest right here in the mountains, where roads cracked, chimneys came down, and homes shifted on their foundations.

The takeaway isn't fear. It's that the damage patterns were predictable, and most of them are the kind you can plan around: things falling, water and power cut off, roads blocked, and older houses moving because they weren't bolted down. Every item below addresses one of those.

Secure Your Space and Store Supplies

The Earthquake Country Alliance, a public-private partnership that includes the USGS and California's emergency management agencies, organizes preparation into Seven Steps to Earthquake Safety. The first is "Secure your space." Walk your house and picture it shaking hard for fifteen seconds. What falls? A water heater that isn't strapped can topple, break a gas line, and cut off a big reservoir of drinkable water all at once. Tall bookcases and dressers can tip onto a bed or a doorway. Heavy mirrors and framed art over the couch become projectiles. Straps, flexible fasteners, and museum putty are cheap, and a Saturday spent anchoring the worst offenders is the highest-value hour in this whole guide.

The third step is to organize disaster supplies, and up here that starts with water. A common preparedness standard is one gallon per person per day, and because mountain roads and utilities can stay down for days after a big quake, more is better. If you're on a well, remember the pump won't run without power, so stored water isn't optional. Keep a few days of non-perishable food, a manual can opener, flashlights, a battery or hand-crank radio, spare batteries, a first-aid kit, any critical medications, and sturdy shoes and work gloves next to each bed for the walk across broken glass in the dark. Our power outage plan for mountain households covers the water, food, and backup-power side in more depth, and most of it applies directly here.

Retrofit and Bracing: Route This to a Pro

Loma Prieta was hard on older houses that weren't fastened to their foundations, and a lot of Boulder Creek's housing stock predates modern seismic standards. A well-established fix exists. The California Residential Mitigation Program, a joint effort of the California Earthquake Authority and state emergency management, runs the Earthquake Brace + Bolt (EBB) program. It describes the retrofit plainly: "the foundation is 'bolted' to the frame of the house, and when there are walls called 'cripple walls' in the crawl space under the house, they are 'braced' with plywood," which helps "prevent the house from sliding or toppling off of its foundation during an earthquake."

The program is limited to "wood-framed, pre-1980 homes with a raised foundation," and when funds are available it offers "up to $3,000 toward a code-compliant seismic retrofit." Income-eligible homeowners may qualify for more. Whether your specific house is a candidate, and how the work should be done, is a question for a licensed contractor or a structural engineer, not a DIY weekend and not this article. Start by checking your eligibility with the program, then get a professional's eyes on your crawl space. This is one of the few home projects that can change whether your house is livable after a major quake.

Learn Drop, Cover, and Hold On

When the ground moves, the goal is simple: don't get knocked down, and don't get hit by what's falling. The Great ShakeOut, run by the same earthquake-safety alliance, gives the three steps. "DROP where you are, onto your hands and knees. This position protects you from being knocked down and reduces your chances of being hit by falling or flying objects." Then "COVER your head and neck with one arm and hand," crawling under a sturdy table if one is close or next to an interior wall if not. Finally, "HOLD ON until the shaking stops," gripping your shelter if you're under one and protecting your head and neck if you're not.

Skip the old advice about doorways and running outside. Most injuries come from moving during the shaking. If you use a wheelchair, the guidance is to "LOCK your wheels (if applicable)," then cover your head and neck with your arms, a book, or a pillow, and hold on. Practice it once with the kids so the motion is automatic, because a real quake is not the moment to think it through.

Sign Up for Earthquake and Emergency Alerts

Two different systems are worth setting up, and they do different jobs.

The first is earthquake early warning. MyShake is a free app built by UC Berkeley's Seismology Lab in partnership with "USGS ShakeAlert and the California Governor's Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES)." It delivers ShakeAlert-powered notifications and is available in the Apple App Store and on Google Play. The point of ShakeAlert is to give you a few seconds of warning before strong shaking arrives, enough time to Drop, Cover, and Hold On. Be clear about what it is and isn't: the USGS states plainly that ShakeAlert "is not earthquake prediction." It detects a quake that has already started and races the alert ahead of the shaking. Alerts can also arrive through your phone's Wireless Emergency Alerts and, on many Android phones, through the built-in Android Earthquake Alerts system.

The second is your county's emergency notifications. CruzAware is Santa Cruz County's alert and warning system, and it's how you'll hear about evacuations, road closures, and other emergencies that often follow a major quake. Register at CruzAware.org and choose how you want to be reached: text, phone, email, or app. While you're on the county's Plan and Prepare pages, it's worth knowing your evacuation zone and grabbing the household safety checklist, since the same plan serves earthquakes, fires, and storms.

Frequently Asked Questions

How close is Boulder Creek to the San Andreas Fault?

Boulder Creek sits within the Santa Cruz Mountains, which the USGS identifies as home to the "Santa Cruz Mountains section" of the San Andreas Fault, a stretch of "62 km (39 mi) from Los Gatos (near San Jose) to San Juan Bautista." The fault marks the boundary between the Pacific and North American plates. For the specific hazard at your address, the USGS earthquake hazards program is the authoritative source.

Was the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake near here?

Yes. The USGS places the magnitude 6.9 quake's epicenter "near Loma Prieta peak in the Santa Cruz Mountains, approximately 9 miles northeast of Santa Cruz," on October 17, 1989. It caused 63 deaths, thousands of injuries, and about $6 billion in damage, and the shaking hit the mountains hard.

Can anyone predict when the next big earthquake will happen?

No. The USGS is direct that its ShakeAlert system "is not earthquake prediction." No credible source can tell you the date of a future quake. Warning systems like ShakeAlert detect a quake that has already begun and send an alert seconds ahead of the shaking. That's why preparation, not prediction, is where your effort belongs.

What's the single most important thing to do first?

Two things, and both take minutes. Install MyShake and sign up for CruzAware so you get warnings and evacuation notices, then strap your water heater and anchor the tallest furniture in your house. After that, work on water storage and a family Drop, Cover, and Hold On practice.

Is my older Boulder Creek house safe in an earthquake?

That depends on your specific house, and it's a question for a professional. Many older homes on raised foundations can be strengthened by bolting the house to the foundation and bracing the cripple walls, work supported by the Earthquake Brace + Bolt program for qualifying pre-1980 homes. Check your eligibility there, then have a licensed contractor or structural engineer assess your crawl space.
seoTitle: Boulder Creek Earthquakes: Fault Lines and How to Prepare
metaDescription: Boulder Creek sits near the San Andreas Fault. Here's the Loma Prieta history, what the shaking risk means, and how to prepare and get alerts.
excerpt: Boulder Creek sits near the San Andreas Fault. Here's the Loma Prieta history, what the shaking risk means, and how to prepare and get alerts.

title: Fire Insurance in the Santa Cruz Mountains: How It Works and Where to Get Help
bodyHtml:
If your homeowners insurer non-renewed you, or your premium jumped to a number that made you sit down, you are not imagining it and you are not alone. Coverage in high-fire areas like ours has gotten harder to find and more expensive, and that has nothing to do with how well you keep your property. This guide explains how the pieces fit together: why insurers pull back, what the California FAIR Plan is, why a FAIR Plan policy usually needs a companion policy, and how home hardening can help. One thing up front, and we mean it plainly: this is general information, not insurance advice. We can't tell you what to buy or what your policy should cost. For that, talk to a licensed insurance agent or broker and to the California Department of Insurance, whose consumer hotline is 800-927-4357.

Why Insurers Are Pulling Back in High-Fire Areas

Santa Cruz County is officially in the high-fire conversation. In its fact sheet Summary on Residential Insurance Policies and the FAIR Plan, the California Department of Insurance lists Santa Cruz among the "counties where 20% or more homes are in high fire risk." That designation is the backdrop to everything a mountain homeowner runs into at renewal time.

The department's data shows the strain. It found that the FAIR Plan, the state's fallback fire insurer, grew "from 1.6% of the residential market to 3.1% of the residential market" through 2022, with the increase "particularly concentrated in counties that have higher fire risk." In other words, more homeowners in places like ours are being pushed out of the regular market and onto the fallback.

Worth a note of caution here, because the cause-and-effect is messier than it looks. The department's own analysis states that its "data does not show a direct relation between wildfire losses and insurance non-renewals and cancellations between 2019 and 2022," and that the year-to-year swings are "likely affected by many factors." So while it's fair to say coverage is tighter in high-fire zones, no one can promise you why any single insurer made any single decision. If you were non-renewed, the reason is specific to your insurer and your policy, and you're entitled to ask them for it in writing.

If You Just Got a Non-Renewal Notice

First, don't panic, and don't let your coverage lapse while you sort it out. A lapse can make you look riskier to the next insurer and can violate your mortgage terms.

Second, check whether you're protected by California's wildfire non-renewal moratorium. After a governor declares a wildfire emergency, a state law can freeze non-renewals in the affected area. In a 2025 example, the Department of Insurance explained that homeowners in and around the ZIP codes covered by a declared wildfire emergency get at least one year of protection from the date of the Governor's emergency declaration, whether or not they suffered a loss, and that homeowners who suffered a total loss get up to 24 months. The protection is tied to specific ZIP codes and a specific declaration, so whether it covers you is a fact to confirm with the Department of Insurance, not an assumption to make.

Third, start shopping early with a licensed agent or broker who knows the mountains. Some brokers specialize in high-fire properties and can reach carriers you won't find on your own. If the regular market truly has nothing for you, that's where the FAIR Plan comes in.

The California FAIR Plan: The Insurer of Last Resort

The California FAIR Plan is not a state agency and it's not a charity. It's an insurance pool that exists as a backstop. In its own words, it "was established so that all California property owners have access to basic fire insurance when access to coverage in the traditional market is not available." It's blunt about its role: "The California FAIR Plan is an insurer of last resort. If you cannot obtain coverage in the traditional insurance market, the California FAIR Plan may be a temporary solution."

Two things to understand. First, it's meant to be temporary, a place to hold coverage while you keep looking for a regular policy, not a permanent home. Second, and this trips up a lot of people, the FAIR Plan's basic policy covers fire and a short list of related perils. It is not a full homeowners policy. That gap is the whole reason the next section exists.

Why a FAIR Plan Policy Usually Isn't Enough on Its Own

A standard homeowners policy bundles a lot together: fire, yes, but also water damage, theft, and personal liability if someone is hurt on your property. The FAIR Plan's basic policy leaves those out. To rebuild something close to full coverage, homeowners pair a FAIR Plan policy with a second policy called a Difference in Conditions, or DIC, policy.

The FAIR Plan explains the split clearly: "DIC policies provide coverages that are not available through the California FAIR Plan, such as water damage, theft and liability coverage," and they're "designed to combine with a California FAIR Plan policy to provide coverage similar to that in a comprehensive homeowner's policy." One important catch: "The California FAIR Plan does not offer DIC policies." You buy the wrap separately, through a licensed agent or broker, and it's a separate contract with its own carrier and its own deductible.

This is where a lot of mountain homeowners get exposed. The Department of Insurance found that only about half of FAIR Plan policyholders actually carry the DIC companion, meaning "many homeowners with FAIR Plan policies do not have an accompanying DIC policy." If you go the FAIR Plan route, treat the DIC wrap as part of the plan, not an optional extra, and ask your agent to quote both together so you can see the full picture.

Home Hardening and Defensible Space Can Help

You can't move your house out of the mountains, but you can make it measurably more resistant to fire, and California rules now tie that work to insurance. Under the Department of Insurance's Safer from Wildfires framework, "Every action under Safer from Wildfires will qualify you for an insurance discount. By doing more, you can save more."

The qualifying actions run from small to significant: a five-foot ember-resistant zone right around the house (the department describes "removing greenery and replacing wood chips with stone or decomposed granite 5 feet around your home"), a Class-A fire-rated roof, ember- and fire-resistant vents, and defensible space that follows state and local law by "trimming trees and removal of brush and debris from yard." Community-wide efforts count too, including recognized Firewise USA and Fire Risk Reduction Communities.

The tree and brush side of this is a big job in a redwood town, and it's worth doing right. Our guide to tree work in Boulder Creek walks through permits, licensing, and how defensible space actually works so you clear the right things without over-cutting, and our tree services directory lists local companies to start from. One limit to be clear about: discounts vary by insurer and by the specifics of your property, and no one can promise a particular saving or that hardening alone will get you renewed. It improves your odds and can lower your bill. That's the accurate claim.

Questions to Ask a Licensed Agent

You'll get further with an agent by asking specific questions than by asking "why is this so expensive." A few worth writing down:

  • Am I currently protected by any wildfire non-renewal moratorium, and when does it end?
  • Have you checked every admitted carrier and the surplus-lines market for my address, or just a couple?
  • If the FAIR Plan is my option, can you quote the FAIR Plan policy and a DIC wrap together, with both deductibles spelled out?
  • Which Safer from Wildfires actions would lower my premium, and by roughly how much for my property?
  • What exactly is and isn't covered, especially for water damage, theft, and liability?
  • Is my dwelling coverage limit enough to actually rebuild at today's mountain construction costs?

Where to Get Help

Two doors are always open. A licensed insurance agent or broker can shop the market and build the FAIR Plan and DIC combination if it comes to that. And the California Department of Insurance helps consumers directly: its hotline is 800-927-4357, and its staff can explain your rights, tell you whether a moratorium covers your ZIP code, and take a complaint if an insurer isn't following the law.

To say it one more time, because it matters: nothing here is insurance, legal, or financial advice, and nothing here promises you a price or a policy. It's a map of how the system works so you can walk into those conversations knowing the terms. The decisions belong to you and the licensed professionals you hire.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the California FAIR Plan?

It's the state's insurer of last resort for basic fire coverage. The FAIR Plan describes itself as a pool that gives property owners "access to basic fire insurance when access to coverage in the traditional market is not available," and calls itself "a temporary solution" for those who can't get a regular policy. It is not a full homeowners policy on its own.

Why do I need a second policy with the FAIR Plan?

Because the FAIR Plan covers fire and related perils but leaves out things a standard policy includes. A Difference in Conditions (DIC) "wrap" policy adds them back. The FAIR Plan says DIC policies cover "water damage, theft and liability coverage" and are "designed to combine with a California FAIR Plan policy." You buy the DIC separately through an agent, since the FAIR Plan doesn't sell it.

My insurer non-renewed me after a nearby fire. Is that allowed?

It depends. California has a non-renewal moratorium that can protect homeowners "living within or adjacent to a declared wildfire emergency," for at least one year from the Governor's emergency declaration. Whether it applies to your address and dates is specific. Confirm with the California Department of Insurance at 800-927-4357 before assuming either way.

Will clearing brush and hardening my home lower my premium?

It can, though results vary. Under the state's Safer from Wildfires framework, "Every action under Safer from Wildfires will qualify you for an insurance discount." The size of the discount depends on your insurer and your property, so no one can promise a specific number. Ask your agent which actions apply to you and keep records of the work.

Who do I call if I can't find any coverage?

Start with a licensed insurance agent or broker who works high-fire properties. If the regular market has nothing, ask them about a FAIR Plan policy plus a DIC wrap. For your rights and for help understanding your options, call the California Department of Insurance consumer hotline at 800-927-4357.
seoTitle: Fire Insurance in the Santa Cruz Mountains: A Plain Guide
metaDescription: Non-renewed or facing a huge premium? Here's how the FAIR Plan, DIC wrap policies, and home hardening work, plus where to get help. Not insurance advice.
excerpt: Non-renewed or facing a huge premium? Here's how the FAIR Plan, DIC wrap policies, and home hardening work, plus where to get help. Not insurance advice.
